questions.questionText: A child with bilateral pitting edema, moon facies, thin sparse hair (flag sign), hepatomegaly, and skin changes ('flaky paint' dermatosis) but preserved subcutaneous fat has:
questions.optionB: Kwashiorkor (protein deficiency with adequate calories — edema, fatty liver, skin/hair changes, preserved fat, emotional apathy)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Kwashiorkor = protein deficiency + edema + preserved fat vs Marasmus = calorie deficiency + wasting. Why correct: Kwashiorkor: bilateral pitting edema (key differentiator), moon facies, flag sign (alternating light/dark bands on hair), fatty hepatomegaly (impaired lipoprotein synthesis), flaky paint dermatosis, apathy, preserved subcutaneous fat. Why A wrong: Marasmus = severe calorie deficiency → growth retardation, emaciation, loss of subcutaneous fat, 'old man' appearance, hungry/alert (not apathetic), NO edema. Why C wrong: Marasmic kwashiorkor = features of both (wasting + edema). Why D wrong: Pellagra = niacin (B3) deficiency → 4 D's (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, death). Clinical relevance: Most common nutritional deficiency globally. Diagnosis: weight-for-height <-3 SD (SAM) or bilateral pitting edema. Treatment: F-75 → F-100 therapeutic feeding. Exam trap: EDEMA differentiates kwashiorkor from marasmus. No edema = marasmus. Edema = kwashiorkor. Memory aid: 'Kwashiorkor = K for Kollosal edema.' Reference: Park – Nutrition.

questions.questionText: Iodine deficiency disorder (IDD) is best measured in a community by:
questions.optionB: Urinary iodine excretion (median urinary iodine concentration >100 μg/L indicates adequate iodine nutrition in a population)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Urinary iodine excretion is the most reliable indicator of current iodine status in a population. Why correct: >90% of dietary iodine is excreted in urine. Population median urinary iodine: <20 μg/L = severe deficiency, 20-49 = moderate, 50-99 = mild, 100-199 = adequate, 200-299 = above requirements. Why A wrong: Serum T4 is variable and difficult to standardize for population surveys. Why C wrong: Neonatal TSH screening is useful but urinary iodine is the primary survey tool. Why D wrong: Clinical goiter rate shows past deficiency (goiter takes time to develop/regress). Clinical relevance: India: NIDDCP (National Iodine Deficiency Disorders Control Programme). Universal salt iodization (USI) — mandatory in India. 15-30 ppm iodine in salt at production. Exam trap: Urinary iodine + goiter rate + neonatal TSH = WHO criteria for IDD monitoring. Urinary iodine = current status; goiter = past exposure. Memory aid: 'Urine shows current iodine; goiter shows history.' Reference: Park – Nutrition.
questions.conceptTag: IDD urinary iodine salt iodization indicator
